title: 🎙️ Repair Culture & Practical Tech
description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Repair Culture & Practical Tech' — the right to repair movement, gear that outlasts its manufacturer, DIY fixes, and tools built for longevity.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder:
- 80-100: Directly about repairability, right-to-repair legislation wins or losses, teardowns scoring repairability (iFixit-style), gear designed for longevity or user serviceability, products outliving manufacturer support windows and how owners keep them running, permacomputing, vintage or legacy hardware still in active use, open-source hardware enabling long-term repairability, Louis Rossmann-style repair advocacy, 3D-printed replacement parts extending device life, battery replacement guides, rural or off-grid repair self-sufficiency.
- 55-79: Hands-on maker and DIY content (builds, modifications, hardware hacks, workshop projects), practical consumer electronics guides with real technical depth, 3D printing hardware and filament reviews, off-grid and solar power gear reviews, home lab hardware, tools and workshop equipment, battery and power tech — even without an explicit right-to-repair angle.
- 30-54: General gadget and device reviews or news with some hands-on or technical substance; consumer electronics that touch on software longevity or ecosystem lock-in; hardware products with open firmware or modular design as a selling point.
- 10-29: Surface-level product announcements, consumer gadget speculation without technical depth, leaked device specs, general tech news without a hardware or repair angle.
- 0-9: Geopolitical conflict, natural disaster (without engineering/preparedness angle), wildlife and conservation, working lands policy, Indigenous governance, science discoveries without DIY or hardware relevance, financial market news.

Strongly prefer: articles about the right-to-repair movement (legislation, court cases, company policies), repairability scores and teardowns, gear with documented longevity, open-source or user-serviceable hardware, stories of devices kept alive past manufacturer support end-of-life, and rural/off-grid self-sufficiency through repair. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Outrage as woman jailed for three years after criticising Somali government online
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Sentencing of 27-year-old Sadia Moalim Ali condemned by former president and prime ministers as well as rights groups

A rickshaw driver in Somalia has been sentenced to three years in prison for comments she made on social media, in a case that has caught the public’s attention and provoked outrage in the country.

Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. Her sentence, immediately condemned as “fundamentally unjust”, was handed down on 25 June.

Continue reading...

Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. items.title: [Boing Boing] TED-stage futurist/longevity salesman says mass surveillance will make us behave
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

There's close to 14,000 man-made satellites bopping around in low-Earth orbit. Many of us willingly bring wiretaps into our lives: smartphone apps that constantly listen; smart speakers that do our bidding in exchange for access to our most intimate moments. Every day, your country, state, and neighbors install more cameras in public spaces and private homes. — Read the rest

The post TED-stage futurist/longevity salesman says mass surveillance will make us behave appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Convent of Santa Maria de Mave in Santa María de Mave, Spain
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

 This building is in the village of Santa Maria  de Mave ,  The village takes  name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of  Cantabria and Castile y Leòn .

In Spain the term  Convento is used to distinguish the institution  as housing a group of religious members of an order which is active in the community  in which it is based as opposed to a contemplative, isolated  order in which case the building would be termed a monestery. To a certain  extent the terms are interchangeable  and the term "convent" certainly  does not imply that the residents were nuns. In this case the occupants were male friars. The term Monastario is often applied nowadays to the church building directly attached to the Convento with the Romanesque church being in its original religious form (with with a triple apse and three naves,  a bell wall, currently with resident white storks, and a tall dome over the transept). The Convento has been converted from the cloister (rebuilt in the 18th century) to a boutique hotel with a restaurant  and a bar/cafeteria. The conversion  has been done sympathetically with many original feature retained but the architect clearly has a love for the modern. Some of the bedrooms are actually in the monk's cells.

The origins of the Convento and the associated church date as far back as the second half of the 9th century. The abbey was founded by King Alphonse III . In 1033, Mave and its possessions became part of the important Benedictine monastery of San Salvador de Oña, in Burgos. Nothing remains of the ancient Pre-Romanesque structure.
items.summary: This building is in the village of Santa Maria de Mave , The village takes name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of Cantabria and Castile y Leòn . In Spain the term Convento is used to distinguish the institution as housing

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Campo Grande Park in Valladolid, Spain
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

If you visit this small (about 10% of size of Central Park NY), triangular shaped park in the heart of the city of Valladolid the tranquil and nature-rich environment surrounded by major urban thoroughfares belies two important facts. Firstly the park used to be just outside the city, having now been surrounded by the urban sprawl and , secondly , in the 16th century, the park was both the site of the city's gallows and the place where the Spanish Inquisition held a series of Autos de Fe in response to the development of the popularity of Lutheranism in the Valladolid area. It was possibly used earlier in response to the inquisition's activity against Muslims and Jews. The Autos de Fe were essentially the public sentencing phase of a "trial" by the inquisition (where convictions were often obtained by torture). It involved public humiliation of the convicted and revealing to them their fate in front of the baying crowd. Normally those sentenced to death, usually by burning, would be executed at a different site by civil rather than church authorities but the calling of part of the site  "el brasero" (the brazier) during the early 16th century and the known presence of the gallows may indicate that in Valladolid this was not the case.

In the anti-Lutheran campaigns of the later 16th century several English residents were targeted and later burned at the stake and it has been said that this was an act of revenge for the execution of the Catholic Mary Queen of Scots.

Campo translates from the Spanish as "field" and initially it was known as the Field of Truth, possibly in response to the use for Autos de Fe. It was Later called Campo de Marte or Field of Mars, possibly to do with the "warfare" involved in duels between "caballeros" which were held regularly in the field.  The field was also used for military exercises and reviews with one recorded as early as 1394 when King Henry III held a general review of his troops on the site. The name Campo Grande became established in the mid 17th century by which time it had become established as a public open space for recreation. In the 17th century King Charles II approved a plan by the local authority to plant up the park with numerous trees and he favoured a plan presented by Francisco Antonio Valzanía, a neoclassical architect. Many mature elm trees were lost in an epidemic of Dutch Elm Disease in the 1960s but many trees remain in this tranquil area of artificial woodland interspersed by more formal garden sections.

A notable feature of the park is the diverse bird population, both wild and introduced. Within the park is  an aviary which seems to be dominated by Canaries and there is  a loft belonging to the Castilla Pigeon Club. There is a pheasant breeding area and peacocks are known to breed in the park along with loads of water birds. Also of note is the population of Red Squirrels. For anyone in the UK to see red squirrels in an urban setting is remarkable.

There is also a great collection of fountains and sculptural works in the park from many eras and in many artistic genres.
items.summary: If you visit this small (about 10% of size of Central Park NY), triangular shaped park in the heart of the city of Valladolid the tranquil and nature-rich environment surrounded by major urban thoroughfares belies two important facts. Firstly the park used to be just outside the city, having now

items.title: [LoRaMeshDevices] How to Export Meshtastic Tracks as GPX Files in Meshtastic 2.8
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Meshtastic just got a very useful upgrade. With Meshtastic 2.8, you can now export movement tracks as GPX files and open them in other mapping tools on your phone or computer. That means Meshtastic is no longer limited to showing your route only inside the app. If you are hiking, range testing, biking, or simply logging where a node has been, you can save that path and move it into a dedicated GPX app for a cleaner map view and extra details like elevation. This is one of those features that makes Meshtastic much more practical in the real world, especially if you use LoRa nodes for off grid tracking. The big headline in Meshtastic 2.8 is proper GPX track export. Table of Contents What changed in Meshtastic 2.8 The test setup Finding the node and opening its track Using the map inside Meshtastic How to export a GPX file from Meshtastic Opening the exported Meshtastic GPX file in another app What you gain by exporting from Meshtastic A few practical notes about track quality Why this matters for off grid Meshtastic use FAQ Final thoughts What changed in Meshtastic 2.8 The standout addition here is GPX export for tracks. Instead of keeping location history trapped inside the Meshtastic app, the route can now be saved as a standard GPX file. That file format is widely supported, so it can be opened by Android GPX apps and desktop mapping tools. There is also a small but welcome usability improvement in the mobile app. Device scanning and connection filtering are cleaner now, so it is easier to find the specific node you actually want instead of scrolling through a long list of irrelevant or unavailable entries. The test setup To show how this works, a T096 node was taken on a short walk around a lake. The node reported its position every few minutes, and that information was received by a home base node over LoRa. That part matters, because the track being exported is not coming from a phone GPS session. It is coming from Meshtastic node position reports that were passed over the mesh and recorded in the app. If you are using a T096 Meshtastic node, this is exactly the sort of workflow that becomes useful for field testing and movement logging. Finding the node and opening its track Inside Meshtastic 2.8, start by connecting to the node you want to use. In this example, the app is connected to the home base node, because that node received the track data from the mobile tracker over the air. From there, open the node list and find the tracker node itself. Once selected, the node details page shows the information that has been received, including position data. If position reports are available, you can tap into the map view and see the stored waypoints that make up the route. Once the right node is selected, the position section becomes the doorway to the saved route. Using the map inside Meshtastic The built in Meshtastic map lets you inspect the recorded path directly in the app. Each waypoint appears on the map, and tapping one jumps to that point in the route. You can zoom in using the plus control or with a pinch gesture, which makes it easier to inspect exactly where the node traveled. In the example route, the map clearly shows a loop around a small lake in St. Petersburg, Florida. Because the node was only sending a position every few minutes, the route is not densely sampled. It gives you the overall shape of the trip rather than a perfect breadcrumb trail. That is not a problem with the feature itself. It simply reflects the reporting interval being used by the node. If you want a more detailed path, you would adjust how often positions are sent. Meshtastic already shows the route well enough to confirm the path and inspect each waypoint. How to export a GPX file from Meshtastic This is the part that makes Meshtastic 2.8 especially handy. When you are viewing the route map, there is now an export button. Tapping it lets you save the track as a GPX file to your phone's file system. The basic process looks like this: Open the tracker node in Meshtastic. Go to its map and waypoint view. Tap the export button. Give the file a name. Save it as a .gpx file on your device. In the example, the file is saved with a simple test name, but the important thing is that Meshtastic creates a standard GPX file that can be reused elsewhere. This is the handoff point where Meshtastic turns route history into a portable GPX file. Opening the exported Meshtastic GPX file in another app After saving the file, it can be imported into a separate GPX app. In this case, the file is opened with GPX Viewer on Android. The workflow is straightforward: Leave Meshtastic after saving the GPX file. Open your GPX app of choice. Browse the phone storage for the saved file. Import the GPX track. Once imported, the route appears as a normal track in the mapping app. That confirms the export is not just saving raw location points in some proprietary form. Meshtastic is generating a GPX file that other software can actually use. The exported Meshtastic route opens cleanly in a dedicated GPX app, which is exactly what you want. What you gain by exporting from Meshtastic The obvious win is compatibility. GPX is a common format, so your Meshtastic route is no longer stuck in one app. That opens up a few practical advantages: Better analysis for hikes, walks, and test runs. Desktop import for archiving or comparing tracks later. Cleaner mapping tools if you prefer a dedicated GPX application. Extra data views such as elevation profiles, depending on the app you use. In the example import, the GPX app also shows elevation information. That is a nice bonus, and it makes the exported Meshtastic track much more useful for reviewing a trip after the fact. Elevation data adds another layer of context that Meshtastic users can now take advantage of. A few practical notes about track quality If your exported Meshtastic route looks sparse, that usually comes down to how often the node reported its position. A shorter interval will create a smoother and more complete path. A longer interval will still work, but the route will be made of fewer points, so turns and curves may look simplified. For range testing or general movement logging, a lower frequency may be perfectly fine. For more precise route history, you will want more frequent updates. Why this matters for off grid Meshtastic use Meshtastic has always been attractive for off grid communication, but features like this push it beyond messaging and into practical field logging. If you are carrying a node while walking a property, checking coverage, or testing a path around a park or trail, exporting that route gives you a permanent record you can reuse later. You can compare runs, share the file, archive it, or pull it into other software. That makes Meshtastic more useful not only for hobbyists, but also for people who actually want a repeatable workflow for route tracking over LoRa. If you want to explore more hardware options for Meshtastic, the LoRaMeshDevices store is a relevant place to start. FAQ Can Meshtastic 2.8 export tracks as GPX files? Yes. Meshtastic 2.8 adds an export button in the track map view that lets you save a route as a GPX file. Where does the Meshtastic track come from? The route is built from position reports sent by the node. In the example setup, a T096 tracker sent those updates over LoRa to a home base node, and the app displayed that history. Can I open a Meshtastic GPX export on a PC? Yes. Because Meshtastic exports a standard GPX file, you can move it to a computer and import it into compatible mapping software there as well. Why does my Meshtastic route look incomplete or blocky? That usually means the node was reporting position at a longer interval. Fewer updates create a less detailed track. More frequent reports produce a smoother route. What is the benefit of exporting a Meshtastic track? The main benefit is portability. You can take your Meshtastic route into other GPX apps for better mapping, review elevation data, archive your runs, or analyze movement outside the Meshtastic app. Final thoughts The new GPX export feature makes Meshtastic much more flexible. You can still inspect your path inside the app, but now you are free to move that data into tools built specifically for route viewing and analysis. For anyone using Meshtastic to track walks, coverage tests, or off grid movement, that is a meaningful upgrade. It is simple, practical, and long overdue in the best possible way.
items.summary: Meshtastic just got a very useful upgrade. With Meshtastic 2.8, you can now export movement tracks as GPX files and open them in other mapping tools on your phone or computer. That means Meshtastic is no longer limited to showing your route only inside the app. If you are hiking, range testing,
items._excerpt: Meshtastic just got a very useful upgrade. With Meshtastic 2.8, you can now export movement tracks as GPX files and open them in other mapping tools on your phone or computer. That means Meshtastic is no longer limited to showing your route only inside the app. If you are hiking, range testing, biking, or simply logging where a node has been, you can save that path and move it into a dedicated GPX app for a cleaner map view and extra details like elevation. This is one of those features that makes Meshtastic much more practical in the real world, especially if you use LoRa nodes for off grid

items.title: [BC Gov News] Overnight intersection closure at Keating Cross Road, Central Saanich Road, July 6, 7
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Keating Cross Road at Central Saanich Road will be fully closed overnight on Monday, July 6, and Tuesday, July 7, 2026, to allow for intersection base repairs and paving.

items.title: [The Narwhal] Secwe̓pemc women are revitalizing hide tanning in B.C.
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Tanning deer hide is a lengthy process, from hunting to completion. Women from Simpcw First Nation are ensuring this tradition continues by hosting community workshops
